name: Royce Gracie

qualityWinLedger.context: Elite UFC 1 tournament opponent, but not yet an established UFC champion; no later-career back-credit.
Elite UFC 4 tournament finalist with real championship-level danger, held below champion-level because the UFC hierarchy was not yet established.
Credible UFC 1 finalist and combat-sport veteran, but not a modern Top-10-equivalent win.
Experienced early tournament opponent with useful but non-ranked UFC value.
UFC 2 finalist and dangerous specialist, but limited established UFC quality at the time.
Legitimate tournament semifinalist with useful early-UFC value, not ranked-equivalent depth.
Dangerous and physically imposing, but essentially unproven in UFC terms entering the fight.
UFC debutant with essentially no established mixed-rules opponent-quality proof.
UFC debutant with minimal established UFC opponent-quality proof.
Memorable early-UFC name, but one novelty-style UFC win did not establish meaningful opponent-quality proof.
Historic martial-arts name, but age and lack of established UFC value sharply limit credit.

apexPeakDetail.notes: The two real wins support a maximum Best-Fighter Claim and maximum Aura without using an aggregate tournament result.

shortCase: The foundational tournament legend: an 11-0-1 opening run, complete finishing dominance, and the résumé that made Brazilian jiu-jitsu impossible to ignore.
counterArgument: He does not rank higher because early tournaments were not the same as a modern UFC title reign, the opponent pool was undeveloped, only two victories receive Top-5-level credit, and his counted elite window lasted roughly 1.4 years. The model respects the dominance without pretending the competitive depth matched later divisions.
whyRankedHere: Gracie ranks here because his early UFC run was historically dominant inside the format that existed. He won three tournaments, opened 11-0-1, finished every UFC victory, and defeated the two strongest established opponents on the ledger in Ken Shamrock and Dan Severn.
whyNotHigher: He does not rank higher because early tournaments were not the same as a modern UFC title reign, the opponent pool was undeveloped, only two victories receive Top-5-level credit, and his counted elite window lasted roughly 1.4 years. The model respects the dominance without pretending the competitive depth matched later divisions.
